topic: DLP4710 Projection Lens Selection for Marin Globe — Friday Feb 27
summary: Offset mounting approach finalized for DLP4710 with elliptical mirror relay system and lens procurement options.

participants.name: Theo
participants.description: Strategic project manager for Marin. Coordinates all workstreams, tracks timeline, manages dependencies, and ensures the Day 30 gate is met.

participants.name: Palmer Luckey
participants.description: Optical engineer for Marin. Founded Oculus VR at 19, designed the Rift optics, founded Anduril Industries. Expert in display optics, Fresnel/TIR lenses, diffusion systems, and perceptual uniformity. Designs the optical stack: LED collimation, diffusion layers, reflective control, and seam blending.

participants.name: Nikola Tesla
participants.description: Electrical engineer for Marin. Designs power system (12-24V DC), STM32H7 control board, LED driver circuits (TLC5940/5971), Ethernet interface, thermal management, and PCB architecture.

participants.name: John Carmack
participants.description: Firmware and software engineer for Marin. Writes STM32H7 firmware (engine addressing, PWM, gamma, thermal), host API (REST/WebSocket), animation engine, OTA updates, and diagnostics.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good morning team. Last standup we selected the mirror relay system for the DLP4710 Marin Globe. Today we need to get specific on lens procurement and mounting. Palmer, I need exact part numbers and positioning specs so we can order hardware and start mechanical design. We're Day 14 of 30 for the gate.

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: Right, so I've been digging into the mirror relay approach. The key insight is we need a two-stage system: first a wide-angle capture lens on the DLP4710, then a convex mirror to redirect that cone outward to fill the sphere. For the capture lens, I'm looking at the Sunex DSL315 - it's a 1.8mm focal length fisheye with 185-degree field of view. About $200. The convex mirror is trickier - we need something like a 25mm diameter spherical mirror with maybe 15mm radius of curvature.

transcript.speaker: Nikola Tesla
transcript.message: Palmer, this two-stage approach concerns me from a positioning standpoint. Where exactly does the DLP4710 EVM mount inside a 6-8 inch sphere? And this convex mirror - how do we power and control its positioning? Is it fixed or adjustable? The mechanical complexity could impact our power budget and thermal design.

transcript.speaker: John Carmack
transcript.message: Tesla raises a good point about positioning. But I'm more worried about the optical path calibration. Palmer, with a two-stage system, how do we handle keystone correction and geometric distortion in firmware? The DLP4710 has built-in correction, but adding a convex mirror introduces non-linear distortions that might be hard to compensate for in real-time.

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: John, you're right about the distortion complexity. But here's the thing - we can pre-calculate the distortion map once we fix the mirror position. It's basically a spherical projection transformation. The real question is whether we mount the DLP4710 at the sphere center pointing up at a mirror on the "north pole," or offset it to one side. Center mounting gives better symmetry but harder access for connections.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Palmer, I need you to make that call today. We can't keep the mechanical design in limbo. What's your recommendation - center mount or offset? And Tesla, can you handle power routing either way?

transcript.speaker: Nikola Tesla
transcript.message: Center mounting would require power and data cables routed through the sphere base, which is doable. But thermal management becomes critical - the DLP4710 generates significant heat and we'd have no natural convection in the center. I'd need active cooling, probably a small fan, which adds noise and power draw. Offset mounting gives us better thermal options.

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: Actually, Tesla's thermal point is decisive. Let's go offset mounting - DLP4710 positioned about 30mm from sphere center, angled up at a mirror positioned on the opposite interior wall. This gives us asymmetric coverage but we can compensate with mirror positioning. And we get better heat dissipation with the projector closer to the shell.

transcript.speaker: John Carmack
transcript.message: Offset works for me - easier geometric transforms than trying to handle center-point singularities anyway. But Palmer, what about coverage uniformity? If we're offset, won't we get hot spots and dark zones? Can the mirror design compensate, or do I need to handle it in software with brightness mapping?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: Both, honestly. The mirror shape can help - maybe not a perfect sphere but slightly elliptical to stretch the coverage. But you'll definitely need software brightness compensation. Think of it like lens vignetting correction in cameras. We measure the actual coverage pattern and build a correction map into the firmware.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Alright, decisions made. Palmer - offset mounting with elliptical mirror. I need specific procurement specs by Monday: exact Sunex lens part number, mirror specifications, mounting hardware. Tesla - design the offset power and thermal system. John - start on distortion correction algorithms. Any blockers I should know about?

transcript.speaker: Nikola Tesla
transcript.message: One blocker - the Sunex DSL315 might need a custom mount adapter for the DLP4710. Their eval board has a standard C-mount but fisheye lenses often use M12 or custom threads. Palmer, can you confirm the mechanical interface?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: Good catch. The DSL315 is C-mount compatible, but I need to verify the back focal distance works with the DLP4710's optical stack. If not, we might need to go with an M12 fisheye alternative like the Lensagon BF2M15025 series. Similar specs, about $150, but different mounting.

transcript.speaker: John Carmack
transcript.message: What's our fallback if the distortion correction proves too computationally expensive? The STM32H7 has decent floating-point performance but real-time geometric transforms on high-resolution content could be challenging. Should I prototype this first or commit to the approach?

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: John, prototype first. Better to know the limits now than discover them at integration. Palmer, I need lens options - both C-mount and M12 alternatives with exact part numbers, pricing, and availability. Tesla, thermal simulations for offset mounting with and without active cooling. We reconvene Monday with concrete specs and prototyping results. This decides our mechanical design direction for the next two weeks.

actionItems.text: Specify exact lens procurement options for DLP4710 fisheye system
Design offset mounting power and thermal management system
Prototype distortion correction algorithms for mirror relay system

topPriorities: Lens procurement specifications and mechanical interface verification

ceoDirectives.directive: What is Lever's direction? → Hybrid — personal tool now with product optionality built in
Keep faith content as separate standalone section (not merged into Family tab)
Keep cash liquid — no deployment until post-launch revenue data
App Store submission: full current build (all 4 tabs) as-is with faith lessons. Submit immediately after merge, ship modal as day-one update.
Paper trading MVP: manual entry (6 weeks), basic free / advanced analytics premium. Integrate into current Lever app finance tab.
Launch with delayed market data for both free and premium tiers. Real-time as future ultra-premium.
Org flattened to 8 C-suite agents. No permanent sub-agents — spawn dynamically via sessions_spawn.
